title-group.article-title: Associations between moderate-to-high physical activity levels and long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, cardiorespiratory fitness, sleep quality, and quality of life in patients with long COVID: a cross-sectional study

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/046h7rx26grid.416121.10000 0004 0573 0539Department of Nursing, Tri-Service General Hospital Songshan Branch, Taipei, Taiwan, R.O.C.

To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Moderate-to-high physical activity (PA) levels have been shown to mitigate health complications, improve cardiovascular health, and enhance patient-reported outcomes, such as sleep quality and quality of life (QoL), among patients with chronic diseases. However, little is known about the associations between moderate-to-high PA levels and long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, cardiorespiratory fitness, sleep quality, and QoL among patients with long COVID. This study aimed to examine whether moderate-to-high PA levels are associated with more favorable outcomes in terms of long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, cardiorespiratory fitness, and QoL, compared to those with low PA levels.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A cross-sectional study was conducted to recruit patients with long COVID (n = 219) from an integrated outpatient clinic for post-COVID-19 at a medical center in northern Taiwan. Eligible participants were categorized into moderate-to-high and low PA groups. PA levels, sleep quality, and QoL were assessed using the International Physical Activity Questionnaire, Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, and World Health Organization Quality of Life Questionnaire-short form, respectively. Cardiorespiratory fitness and heart rate recovery were evaluated using cycle ergometer-based graded exercise tests. Multivariate linear and logistic regression models were used to evaluate the associations between PA levels, long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, sleep quality, and QoL.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Participants with a mean age of 48.7 years had a mean duration of post-COVID-19 of 12.3 weeks. Patients with moderate-to-high PA levels had a borderline lower risk of shortness of breath (OR = 0.44, p = 0.065), significantly greater VO₂ peak (B = 4.68, p < 0.001) and 2-minute heart rate recovery (B = 3.79, p = 0.049), better sleep quality (B = -1.68, p = 0.007), and higher scores in the physical (B = 0.99, p = 0.023) and psychological (B = 1.11, p = 0.030) domains of QoL, compared to those with lower PA levels.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Maintaining moderate-to-high PA levels among patients with long COVID may be associated with reduced symptom distress, greater cardiorespiratory fitness and heart rate recovery, and better sleep quality, which are in turn linked to better QoL.

sec.title: Clinical trial number
sec.p: not applicable.

funding-source.institution: Ministry of National Defense-Medical Affairs Bureau

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection, known as long COVID, is characterized by persistent, relapsing, or new symptoms that develop or continue for at least months or even years after the initial infection, representing a significant global clinical and public health challenge [1]. An estimated 10% of individuals infected with SARS-CoV-2 experience a range of persistent and varied symptoms [2, 3], commonly referred to as long COVID symptoms, including shortness of breath, chest pain, heart palpitations, sleep disturbances, and psychological issues, such as anxiety and depression, which can result in reduced quality of life (QoL) [4, 5]. A recent meta-analysis found that the overall prevalence of poor QoL was 59% among patients with long COVID, particularly those who were admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) and those reporting fatigue [6]. Persistent symptoms, particularly fatigue and dyspnea, have significantly negative psychological impacts, such as anxiety, depression, or post-traumatic stress disorder, which collectively lead to a diminished QoL. In addition, the ongoing physical challenges posed by these symptoms exacerbate psychological distress, contributing to a vicious cycle that further impairs the overall well-being and recovery of patients with long COVID. Therefore, addressing the physical and psychological aspects of long COVID is essential for improving patient outcomes and enhancing QoL [7, 8]. Furthermore, the long-term impacts of COVID on QoL are being increasingly recognized, thereby highlighting the need for comprehensive healthcare responses. By exploring the predictors or factors associated with better QoL, we can develop strategies to improve the QoL of patients with long COVID.

Sleep disturbance, one of the most prevalent symptoms among patients with long COVID, may raise the demand for further exploration of associated factors and intervention strategies. An observational study found that nearly one-fifth of patients with long COVID suffered from sleep disturbance [9]. Moreover, a systematic review and meta-analysis revealed that the overall pooled prevalence of sleep disturbances among patients with long COVID was 46%, with subgroup analyses indicating a pooled prevalence of reduced sleep quality at 56%, insomnia at 38%, and excessive daytime sleepiness at 14% [10]. These findings highlight that sleep disturbances are prevalent among patients with long COVID and require early implementation of effective interventions to mitigate the potential long-term consequences associated with sleep problems.

Engaging in regular physical activity (PA) is increasingly being recognized as a pivotal component in the management and prevention of chronic diseases. It has been documented that moderate-to-high PA levels alleviate various health complications associated with chronic conditions (including those experienced by patients with long COVID), enhance cardiovascular health, and improve patient-reported outcomes [11–13]. Encouraging patients with long COVID to engage in tailored PA regimens may help mitigate some of those symptoms and improve their QoL. An observational study found that patients with long COVID exhibited significantly lower levels of moderate-to-high PA than healthy individuals [14]. Additionally, prolonged sedentary behavior was independently linked to a higher risk of acute COVID-19 and long COVID, while physical inactivity played contradictory roles among patients with long COVID [14]. A recent systematic review with meta-analysis indicated that engaging in moderate-to-high PA levels may effectively reduce fatigue, anxiety, and depression while also improving sleep quality among patients with long COVID [15]. In addition, physical exercise-based rehabilitation has been shown to alleviate long COVID symptoms, such as dyspnea, fatigue, and depression, alongside enhancing performance on the 6-minute walk test and overall QoL. This approach represents a promising therapeutic strategy that can be integrated into routine clinical practice for patients recovering from COVID-19 [16].

However, despite a growing body of evidence supporting the benefits of moderate-to-high PA levels in alleviating long COVID symptoms, significant gaps exist in our understanding of the effects of different PA levels on heart rate recovery, sleep quality, and overall QoL among individuals with long COVID. Although previous studies have highlighted the prevalence of persistent symptoms and the value of engagement in increased PA levels, comprehensive research examining the direct relationship between PA and heart rate recovery—an essential measure of cardiovascular health—is lacking. In addition, much of the existing literature tends to focus on general symptom relief without thoroughly investigating the associations of various PA levels with sleep quality and QoL among patients with long COVID. Therefore, this study addressed the aforementioned gaps by examining the connections between moderate-to-high PA levels and heart rate recovery, sleep quality, and QoL, thereby providing valuable insights for the development of tailored therapeutic strategies to effectively manage long COVID symptoms.
This study aimed to investigate the association between PA levels and long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, cardiorespiratory fitness, and QoL among individuals with long COVID.

By exploring these aspects, this study contributes to the development of targeted interventions and support strategies that leverage the benefits of moderate-to-high PA to improve QoL and facilitate the recovery of individuals affected by post-COVID conditions.

sec.title: Study design and setting
sec.p: A cross-sectional study was conducted from May 2022 to April 2023 and involved the recruitment of patients with long COVID from an integrated outpatient clinic at a medical center in northern Taiwan. This study adhered to the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology guidelines.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Two hundred and nineteen eligible participants were screened by a research nurse and confirmed by a physician. The inclusion criteria were as follows: (1) participants who had previously been diagnosed with COVID-19 based on a positive rapid antigen or polymerase chain reaction test [17] and experienced persistent symptoms—such as dyspnea, cough, fatigue, headache, or joint pain—for at least four weeks following a negative rapid antigen test [18]; (2) could communicate in Mandarin; and (3) agreed to participate in the study and provided informed consent. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) individuals with the inability to undergo cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) with contraindications according to the American College of Sports Medicine [19]; (2) those with a history of end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis and those diagnosed with cancer within the past six months; and (3) those with the inability to walk without assistance.

The sample size was estimated using G*Power software (version 3.1.9.7, Germany) for a multiple linear regression model. Assuming a small effect size (f² = 0.1), based on a long COVID prevalence of 10% in the general population [20], to ensure sufficient power for detecting associations [21], with an alpha level of 0.05, statistical power of 80%, and 15 predictors [3, 22, 23], the required sample size was calculated to be 213.

sec.title: Measures
sec.p: Data were collected using questionnaires and CPET by a research assistant and rehabilitation physician.

sec.title: Demographic and clinical characteristics
sec.p: Participants’ demographic and clinical characteristics included age, gender, duration of post COVID-19 infection, height, weight, body mass index (BMI), comorbidities, and smoking status.

sec.title: Physical activity levels
sec.p: The Chinese version of the International Physical Activity Questionnaire (IPAQ) short form, which has well-established reliability and validity, was used to assess the participants’ PA levels over the past week [24]. This 7-item instrument measures the minutes spent on PA, including housework, transportation, leisure activities, and moderate-to-vigorous PA, over the past seven days. The IPAQ scores, which are expressed as “metabolic equivalent (MET)-minutes/week,” were calculated based on the MET level (i.e., walking = 3.3 MET, moderate-intensity PA = 4.0 MET, vigorous-intensity PA = 8.0 MET) multiplied by the minutes of each activity per week [25]. In addition, the total amount of PA was categorized into low and moderate-to-high PA levels based on the MET-minutes/week criterion cutoff point of 600 MET-minutes/week [26, 27].

sec.title: Long COVID symptoms
sec.p: The subjective long COVID symptoms experienced by the participants, including shortness of breath, chest pain, cough, dizziness, headache, fatigue, sleep disturbance, palpitations, brain fog, depression/anxiety, and olfactory dysfunction, were assessed by a physician using a checklist with a binary response (present or absent) following the World Health Organization (WHO) criteria [25]. The WHO announced that post COVID-19 conditions occur among individuals with a history of probable or confirmed SARSCoV-2 infection, usually three months from the onset of COVID-19, with symptoms that last for at least two months and cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Common long COVID symptoms may be newly onset following initial recovery from an acute COVID-19 episode, persist from the initial illness, or fluctuate and relapse over time [1, 28–30].

sec.title: Heart rate recovery and cardiorespiratory fitness
sec.p: The participants’ heart rate recovery and cardiorespiratory fitness were assessed using CPET. The test, supervised by a rehabilitation/sports medicine physician and a research assistant/nurse, was performed using a cycle ergometer (Corival, Lode, Groningen, The Netherlands) with a graded exercise test protocol in which resistance was increased by 10 watts per minute at a constant cycling speed of 60 revolutions per minute. During the test, 1-minute and 2-minute heart rate recovery, cardiorespiratory fitness (peak oxygen uptake [VO2peak]), and lung function parameters—including forced vital capacity (FVC), forced expiratory volume in 1 s (FEV1), FEV1/FVC ratio, and ventilatory efficiency (VE/VCO2 slope)—were measured. Participants’ perceived exertion (assessed using the Borg scale), electrocardiography, heart rate, blood pressure, and peripheral oxygen saturation were continuously monitored to ensure safety during the test. Heart rate recovery, which is associated with all-cause mortality risk [19, 31] was calculated as the difference in heart rate between baseline and 1- and 2-minute post- CPET and presented as 1-minute and 2-minute heart rate recovery.

sec.title: Sleep quality
sec.p: The Taiwanese version of the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, originally developed in 1989 by Dr. Daniel J. Buysse and colleagues at the University of Pittsburgh [32, 33] and translated by Professor Pei-Shan Tsai in Taiwan [34], was used to assess sleep quality. This 19-item scale has demonstrated good validity and reliability in evaluating various aspects of sleep, including subjective sleep quality, sleep duration, sleep latency, sleep efficiency, disturbances, daytime dysfunction, and medication use. Each domain is scored from 0 to 3 points, with the total score ranging from 0 to 21 points. Higher scores indicate poorer sleep quality.

sec.title: Quality of life
sec.p: The Taiwanese version of the World Health Organization Quality of Life Questionnaire short form was used to assess participants’ QoL [35, 36]. This 28-item instrument assesses four specific QoL domains, including physical health, psychological health, social relationships, and environment. Each item is scored from 1 to 5 points, and the domain scores are calculated by multiplying the average score of the items within each domain by a scaling factor of 4, resulting in domain scores ranging from 4 to 20. Higher scores indicate better QoL.

sec.title: Statistical methods
sec.p: Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS (version 24.0; IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Descriptive statistics, including means and standard deviations (SD) or numbers and percentages (%) for the study participants, were presented. The Chi-squared or Student’s t-tests were used to compare the differences between the groups (moderate-to-high vs. low PA levels) among the participants with long COVID, specifically in terms of age, gender, duration of post-COVID-19 symptoms, BMI, comorbidities (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease), and smoking status. Multivariate linear and logistic regression models are statistical approaches that can simultaneously examine the independent effects of multiple predictors on continuous or binary outcomes, adjust for potential confounders/covariates, and provide more precise and reliable estimates of associations in complex real-world data [37]. Accordingly, we applied these models to evaluate the associations between PA levels (independent variable) and outcomes of interest (dependent variables) including long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, sleep quality, and QoL after adjusting for potential covariates: age, gender, duration of post-COVID-19 symptoms, BMI, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and smoking status [38, 39]. In addition, multivariate linear regression analyses were performed to identify factors influencing the participants’ QoL. Covariates were selected based on theoretical and clinical relevance, as well as statistical significance in univariate analyses. All tests were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05.

sec.title: Characteristics of the participants
sec.p: A total of 256 initially approached participants were screened and invited by a research assistant. Thirty-five participants declined to participate in the study because of lack of time (n = 12) and refusal to undergo exercise testing (n = 23). Two patients did not meet the inclusion criteria, while one had a history of cancer and was undergoing chemotherapy and radiotherapy (n = 1), and one had a recent history of lower extremity bone fracture, leading to the inability to undergo exercise testing (n = 1). Ultimately, 219 eligible participants diagnosed with long COVID completed the data collection process.

The mean age of the participants was 48.7 years (SD = 13.0), with a mean duration of post-COVID-19 symptoms of 12.3 weeks (SD = 7.2). Table 1 presents the demographics, including age, gender, duration of post-COVID-19, BMI, comorbidities, and smoking status between the low (n = 33, less than 600 MET-minutes/week) and moderate-to-high PA (n = 186, at least 600 MET-minutes/week) level groups, revealing that there were no significant differences between groups.

caption.p: Comparisons of Demographic and Clinical Characteristics, Long COVID Symptoms, Cardiorespiratory Fitness, Heart rate Recovery, Sleep Quality, and QoL between Low and Moderate-to-high PA levels

table-wrap-foot.p: Data are presented as n (%) or mean ± SD; aThe physical activity levels are categorized as low and moderate-to-high: (1) low: less than 600 MET-minutes/week); (2) moderate-to-high: at least 600 MET-minutes/week; p-values are from either the independent t-test or chi-squared test

sec.title: Associations between PA levels, long COVID symptoms, heart rate recovery, cardiorespiratory fitness, sleep quality, and QoL
sec.p: The prevalence of long COVID symptoms was compared between groups, showing that patients with moderate-to-high PA levels had a reduced prevalence of shortness of breath (28.0% vs. 48.5%, χ2 = 5.52, p = 0.019) compared to those with low PA levels. No differences were observed in the prevalence of other long COVID symptoms between the groups, including chest pain, cough, dizziness, headache, fatigue, sleep disturbance, palpitations, brain fog, depression/anxiety, and olfactory dysfunction (Table 1).

In the multivariate analysis, moderate-to-high PA levels were associated only with borderline significantly reduced odds of experiencing shortness of breath (OR = 0.44, p = 0.065) after adjusting for potential covariates, despite a significant association with lower odds of experiencing shortness of breath (OR = 0.41, p = 0.021), as observed in the univariate analysis (Table 2).Table 2
Associations between Physical Activity Levels and Long COVID Symptoms
Dependent variableUnivariate analysisMultivariate analysis
OR95% C.I.pOR95% C.I.pa
LowerUpperLowerUpper
Long COVID Symptoms
Shortness of breath0.410.190.880.0210.440.191.050.065
Chest pain0.590.281.240.1620.670.291.560.351
Cough0.630.291.380.2520.580.241.390.223
Dizziness0.870.332.280.7700.770.272.170.617
Headache0.850.381.900.6850.820.322.110.686
Fatigue1.330.612.880.4751.240.513.040.639
Sleep disturbance0.960.452.080.9230.870.372.030.739
Palpitations0.580.241.410.2260.570.211.590.285
Brain fog0.930.422.040.8550.860.352.080.732
Depression/anxiety0.760.292.020.5881.470.405.450.564
Olfactory dysfunction0.760.292.020.5880.850.282.630.779
p-values are derived from logistic regression models;aLogistic regression models, with PA levels as the independent variable and long COVID symptoms (binary outcomes) as the dependent variables, are adjusted for demographic and clinical covariates, including age, gender, duration of post-COVID-19, BMI, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and smoking status.

Patients with moderate-to-high PA levels demonstrated higher cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2peak = 26.6 vs. 22.0 ml/kg/min, t = −3.67, p < 0.001) and FEV1 (3.5 vs. 3.2 L/min, t = −2.05, p = 0.042) compared to those with low PA levels. Additionally, the moderate-to-high PA level group exhibited greater 2-min heart rate recovery (28.8 vs. 24.4 beats, t = −2.30, p = 0.023) compared to the low PA level group (Table 1). The results of the multivariate analysis indicated that a moderate-to-high PA level was associated with higher VO₂ peak (B = 4.68, p < 0.001), FVC (B = 0.36, p = 0.033) and FEV1 (B = 0.31, p = 0.021), after adjusting for potential covariates (Table 4). Furthermore, a moderate-to-high PA level was associated with greater 2-min heart rate recovery in the univariate (B = 4.48, p = 0.023) and multivariate analysis models (B = 3.79, p = 0.049, Table 3).Table 3
Associations between Physical Activity Levels, Cardiorespiratory Fitness, Heart Rate Recovery, Sleep Quality, and QoL
Dependent variableUnivariate analysisMultivariate analysis
B95% C.I.pB95% C.I.pa
LowerUpperLowerUpper
Cardiorespiratory fitness
VO2peak(ml/kg/min)4.592.127.06<0.0014.682.347.03<.001
FVC (L/min)0.37-0.010.750.0580.360.030.690.033
FEV1 (L/min)0.340.010.670.0420.310.050.580.021
FEV1/FVC (%)1.46-2.815.730.5010.98-3.895.850.691
VE/VCO2 slope-0.14-1.571.300.851-0.17-1.551.220.811
Heart rate recovery
1 min (beat)2.11-0.915.130.1701.49-1.764.750.366
2 min (beat)4.480.638.330.0233.790.037.550.049
Sleep quality-1.68-2.90-0.450.007-1.33-2.740.080.064
QoL
Physical1.170.371.960.0040.990.141.850.023
Psychological1.230.352.120.0071.110.112.100.030
Social 0.51-0.211.240.1640.48-0.321.270.236
Environmental0.49-0.241.210.1880.45-0.381.280.281
p-values are derived from linear regression models; aLinear regression models, with PA levels as the independent variable and continuous outcomes (VO₂ peak, heart rate recovery, sleep quality, and QoL) as the dependent variables, are adjusted for age, gender, duration of post-COVID-19, BMI, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and smoking status.

Table 1 shows that patients with moderate-to-high PA levels reported better sleep quality (9.0 vs. 10.7, t = 2.21, p = 0.034) and overall QoL (13.7 vs. 12.9, t = −2.65, p = 0.009), particularly in the physical (t = −2.88, p = 0.004) and psychological (t = −2.74, p = 0.007) domains of QoL, compared to those with low PA levels. The multivariate analysis results revealed that moderate-to-high PA levels were only borderline significantly associated with better sleep quality (B = −1.33, p = 0.064) after adjusting for potential covariates, despite a significant association observed in the univariate analysis (B = −1.68, p = 0.007; Table 3). In addition, both univariate and multivariate analyses showed that moderate PA levels were positively associated with better physical and psychological domains of QoL.

sec.title: Factors associated with quality of life among patients with long COVID
sec.p:
Regression analyses were conducted to investigate the factors associated with QoL among patients with long COVID. As presented in Table 4, higher PA levels (β = 0.18, p = 0.009), higher cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2 peak, β = 0.35, p < 0.001), greater 1-min (β = 0.14, p = 0.039) and 2-min heart rate recovery (β = 0.19, p = 0.004), and better sleep quality (β = − 0.34, p < 0.001) were associated with better QoL among patients with long COVID in the univariate analysis. However, after adjusting for the significant covariates, the multivariate analysis revealed that PA levels were not associated with QoL, whereas both cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2 peak, β = 0.281, p < 0.001) and sleep quality (β = − 0.268, p < 0.001) emerged as significant independent factors associated with QoL among patients with long COVID.Table 4
Factors Associated with QoL among Patients with Long COVID based on Multivariate Regression Analysis

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our results emphasize the crucial role of maintaining moderate-to-high PA levels in enhancing the health outcomes of patients with long COVID. Specifically, we found that moderate-to-high PA levels were associated with a less prevalence of shortness of breath, which is consistent with the results of previous research showing that dyspnea is associated with lower PA levels among community-dwelling older adults [40]. A systematic review and meta-analysis also highlighted that pulmonary rehabilitation has beneficial effects on the lower prevalence of dyspnea among patients with long COVID [41]. In a large retrospective cohort study, nearly half of COVID-19 survivors met the recommended levels of moderate-to-vigorous PA, and those reporting persistent symptoms such as shortness of breath were less likely to engage in sufficient PA [42]. Moreover, a recent systematic review and meta-analysis concluded that physical exercise-based rehabilitation had beneficial effects on long COVID-related symptoms characterized by dyspnea, fatigue, and depression among people who had COVID [16]. Our findings support those of the existing literature by suggesting that moderate-to-high levels of PA are associated with a lower prevalence of long COVID-related symptoms and more favorable health outcomes among individuals recovering from COVID-19. Existing evidence also indicates that moderate-to-high PA levels are correlated with fewer symptoms, whereas low PA levels are associated with an increased risk of developing long COVID symptoms. These findings highlight the importance of maintaining an active lifestyle and incorporating moderate-to-high PA-based rehabilitation, particularly for patients with, or those recovering from, COVID-19.

The positive association observed between PA levels and QoL among patients with long COVID aligns with broader research findings regarding the impact of PA on overall well-being. A population-based pooled study demonstrated a robust dose–response relationship between moderate-to-vigorous PA and QoL in large, general population samples. However, the curvilinear nature of this relationship, as emphasized in the study, highlights the importance of finding a sustainable level of PA suitable for each patient with long COVID [43].

Additionally, the findings demonstrate that individuals engaging in moderate-to-high PA levels had greater 2-minute heart rate recovery. Reduced heart rate recovery or heart rate variability can indicate conditions such as cardiac autonomic dysfunction and has been associated with a higher risk of mortality [44]. Moreover, a recent study indicated that long COVID is associated with reduced heart rate variability both at rest and during deep breathing [45]. By contrast, moderate-to-high PA levels were associated with better heart rate recovery, which may reflect a relationship between PA and cardiovascular function among individuals with long COVID.

The strength of the current study lies in its use of CPET to accurately evaluate heart rate recovery and cardiorespiratory fitness in a large sample of patients with long COVID. Our study identified cardiorespiratory fitness and sleep quality as significant factors influencing QoL among patients with long COVID. The decline in cardiorespiratory fitness among COVID-19 survivors is a significant concern that affects their overall recovery and QoL. A systematic review and meta-analysis indicated that pooled effect estimates demonstrated a significant reduction in VO2peak among patients who survived COVID-19 compared with those in the non-COVID-19 group. Additionally, older COVID-19 survivors, particularly those who experienced severe symptoms, were likely to show more pronounced declines in cardiorespiratory fitness [46]. Moura et al. [47] found that higher pre-pandemic cardiorespiratory fitness was associated with lower perceived stress, fewer depressive symptoms, and better QoL during the COVID-19 pandemic among older adults with hypertension. These findings suggest a protective effect of better baseline cardiorespiratory fitness on mental health and QoL outcomes in the context of pandemic-related stressors. Previous studies have linked reduced cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2peak) in COVID-19 ICU survivors with poorer physical functioning. These findings highlight the impact of impaired cardiorespiratory fitness on recovery and QoL. By using CPET, our study provides an objective assessment of cardiorespiratory fitness and confirms its positive association with QoL among individuals with long COVID [48]. Future research should investigate the specific impact of moderate-to-high PA on long COVID outcomes and consider potential interactions with other variables.

Furthermore, moderate-to-high PA levels have been associated with better cardiorespiratory fitness, which is consistent with the findings of the current study [49]. Better cardiorespiratory fitness has also been associated with improved sleep quality and QoL, highlighting the relevance of exploring factors related to cardiorespiratory fitness among patients with long COVID [50].

This study emphasized that maintaining moderate-to-high PA levels among patients with long COVID is associated with better sleep quality and lower symptom interference. Sleep disturbance is a significant concern for patients with long COVID, affecting their overall well-being and daily functioning. Research has shown that those individuals experience a range of sleep-related issues, including poor sleep quality, daytime sleepiness, sleep inertia, higher napping, insomnia, sleep apnea, and nightmares. Notably, the numbers of long COVID symptoms, psychological factors, and age have been identified as strong predictors of these disturbances [51]. Additional findings indicate that individuals with reduced sleep quality and those with shorter sleep durations face a significantly higher risk of experiencing nearly all long-term symptoms one to three months after surviving COVID [51]. Given these findings, promoting regular PA among patients with long COVID is associated with better sleep quality and lower symptom interference. The current study identified cardiorespiratory fitness and sleep quality as significant factors associated with QoL among patients with long COVID. The QoL of these patients is significantly compromised, with a reported prevalence of poor QoL at 59%, particularly among those with a history of ICU admission and fatigue [6]. Therefore, maintaining a moderate-to-high PA level is essential because of its association with higher cardiorespiratory fitness and better sleep quality, ultimately benefiting patients’ overall QoL.

Although the proportion of smokers was higher in the moderate-to-high PA group than in the low-PA group, this difference was not statistically significant. At first glance, this finding may appear counterintuitive given the well-established health risks of smoking. However, a systematic review by Kaczynski et al. [52] found that while approximately 60% of studies reported a negative association between smoking and PA, this relationship was not consistent across all populations. Specifically, the inverse association was often attenuated or even reversed among adolescents and individuals engaging in moderate-to-vigorous intensity exercise. These findings suggest that the relationship between smoking behavior and PA may vary by age, population characteristics, and context, potentially explaining the distribution observed in our sample. Moreover, cultural and psychosocial factors influencing health behaviors in post-COVID populations may also contribute to this pattern and merit further investigation.

sec.title: Limitations and future research directions
sec.p: Acknowledging the limitations that may have influenced the interpretation and generalizability of the results is crucial. First, the study’s cross-sectional design restricts the ability to establish causal relationships. While it indicates a correlation between cardiorespiratory fitness and sleep quality and QoL, it cannot confirm that improvements in these areas directly lead to enhancements of QoL. Second, the study sample consisted of 219 participants drawn from a single medical center in northern Taiwan that specializes in long COVID outpatient care. This limited sampling may hinder the generalizability of the findings because the results may not accurately represent the experiences or characteristics of the entire population of patients with long COVID. Third, although multiple bivariate comparisons using chi-squared and t-tests were performed, no formal adjustment for multiple testing was applied due to the exploratory nature of these analyses. Therefore, the results should be interpreted with caution regarding the potential risk of Type I error. Additionally, non-responder analysis was not conducted due to the lack of recorded demographic data for individuals who declined to participate, which may have introduced potential self-selection bias. Moreover, data on pre-infection PA levels—an important factor potentially influencing recovery—were not collected due to the risk of recall bias. Hence, the interpretation of these results warrants caution.

Data on certain sociodemographic variables, such as education level and income, were not collected in this study. These indicators of socioeconomic status may have influenced individuals’ engagement in PA and their QoL. Future research should incorporate a more comprehensive range of sociodemographic characteristics to better capture potential confounding factors and improve explanatory power. Moreover, the reliance on self-reported data also introduces potential biases in the study. The participants provided information on their PA levels, sleep quality, and overall QoL through questionnaires. Self-reporting can be inherently flawed as individuals may overestimate or underestimate their behaviors and feelings, which can affect the accuracy of the findings. However, we recruited a larger sample compared with previous studies and utilized validated methods, such as CPET, to accurately assess heart rate recovery and cardiorespiratory fitness among participants with long COVID. Future research should prioritize more rigorous methodologies, such as randomized controlled trials, to gain a clearer understanding of how PA affects patients with long COVID, thereby providing a more comprehensive view of this patient population. Additionally, implementing long-term follow-up studies will enable the evaluation of the sustained benefits of PA, ultimately leading to better-informed recommendations for intervention strategies. Finally, the imbalance in PA group sizes, particularly the smaller representation of participants in the low-PA group, may have introduced bias or reduced statistical power. Although multivariate models were adjusted for relevant covariates (e.g., age, sex, BMI, smoking status, comorbidities), residual confounding cannot be fully excluded. Additionally, PA levels were based on self-reported data from the IPAQ-SF, which may overestimate actual PA due to recall and social desirability bias, potentially leading to misclassification. These limitations suggest that the findings should be interpreted with caution, and future studies using objective PA measures (e.g., accelerometers) and more balanced sampling strategies are recommended.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This pioneering cross-sectional study demonstrated that moderate-to-high PA levels are associated with fewer long COVID symptoms, greater heart rate recovery and cardiorespiratory fitness, and better sleep quality as well as QoL. Among patients with long COVID, cardiorespiratory fitness and sleep quality were independently associated with QoL. The results of the current study can serve as a reference for future research focusing on the promotion of PA in this population, particularly among high-risk groups.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Shang-Lin Chiang and Chia-Huei Lin contributed equally to this study.
An article note was added to the published article.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We are grateful to the participants of this study, including the doctors, nursing staff, and medical personnel for their support during the research process.

sec.title: Clinical trial number
sec.p: Not applicable.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: C.-H.L., S.-L.C., and W.-C.T. conceptualized and designed the study method. Y.-P.L., C.-H.L., S.-L.C., and C.-C.C. collected and interpreted the data. C.-H.L. formally analyzed the data; C-H Lin and W.-C.T. visualized and supervised the study. S.-L.C. administered the project. C.-H.L. and S.-L.C. acquired funding. Y.-P.L., C.-H.L., and C.-Y.S. drafted the original manuscript. All authors read, revised, and approved the final manuscript for submission.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by grants from the Tri-Service General Hospital, Taiwan [grant numbers TSGH-D-112176, TSGH-E-112249, TSGH-E-113279 and TSGH-E-114273] and the Ministry of National Defense-Medical Affairs Bureau [grant number MND MAB-D-113085 and MND-MAB-D-114120].

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was conducted in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. Institutional review board approval (TSGHIRB: A202105159) was obtained from the medical center where this study was conducted. All participants provided written informed consent when they were invited to join the study and were assured that their participation was entirely voluntary and that they could withdraw at any time.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
